t: Shape A is covered by 6 square units. Shape B is covered by 9 square units. Which shape has a bigger area?
e: 9 > 6, so Shape B has a bigger area!

t: Shape A has 10 square units. Shape B has 10 square units. What can you say about their areas?
e: Both shapes have 10 square units, so they have the same area! They can look different but still have equal areas.

t: A rug covers 12 square units. A mat covers 8 square units. How many more square units does the rug cover?
e: 12 - 8 = 4. The rug covers 4 more square units than the mat.

t: A rectangle is 5 squares long and 2 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 2 rows of 5 = 10 square units!

t: Which has a bigger area: a shape with 14 square units or a shape with 11 square units?
e: 14 > 11, so the shape with 14 square units has a bigger area!

t: A rectangle is 3 squares long and 3 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 3 rows of 3 = 9 square units!

t: A garden has an area of 15 square units. A patio has an area of 9 square units. How much bigger is the garden?
e: 15 - 9 = 6. The garden is 6 square units bigger!

t: A rectangle is 4 squares long and 2 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 2 rows of 4 = 8 square units!

t: Shape A has an area of 7 square units. Shape B has an area of ___ square units. Together they cover 13. How big is Shape B?
e: 13 - 7 = 6. Shape B has an area of 6 square units!

t: A rectangle is 6 squares long and 2 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 2 rows of 6 = 12 square units!

t: Which has a smaller area: a shape with 5 square units or a shape with 8 square units?
e: 5 < 8, so the shape with 5 square units has a smaller area!

t: A poster covers 16 square units. A card covers 4 square units. How many more square units does the poster cover?
e: 16 - 4 = 12. The poster covers 12 more square units!

t: A rectangle is 5 squares long and 3 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 3 rows of 5 = 15 square units!

t: A shape has an area of ___ square units. It is 3 more than 9. What is the area?
e: 9 + 3 = 12. The area is 12 square units!

t: Two shapes each have an area of 8 square units. What is the total area?
e: 8 + 8 = 16 square units total!

t: A rectangle is 4 squares long and 3 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 3 rows of 4 = 12 square units!

t: Shape A has 11 square units. Shape B has 15 square units. Which is smaller and by how much?
e: 15 - 11 = 4. Shape A is smaller by 4 square units!

t: A rectangle is 7 squares long and 2 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 2 rows of 7 = 14 square units!

t: A table is covered by 20 square tiles. A desk is covered by 12 square tiles. How many more tiles cover the table?
e: 20 - 12 = 8. The table needs 8 more tiles than the desk!

t: A rectangle is 3 squares long and 2 squares wide. What is its area?
e: Count the squares: 2 rows of 3 = 6 square units!
